s: Neo-Gothic palace built to outshine Oxford — and almost manages it
w: Enriqueta Rylands built this neo-Gothic palace on Deansgate as a memorial to her husband, opening it to the public in 1900. Designed by Basil Champneys, its vaulted reading room rivals the Bodleian. Inside: a Gutenberg Bible and a 2nd-century papyrus of St John. Victorian money spending its way to eternity.
_facts: Opened to the public in 1900, founded by Enriqueta Augustina Rylands in memory of John Rylands
Late-Victorian neo-Gothic building designed by Basil Champneys on Deansgate
Houses the third-largest academic library in the UK, now part of University of Manchester
Collections include a Gutenberg Bible and one of the earliest known New Testament fragments

s: The most refined little building in Manchester — a private library since 1806
w: Built 1802-06 in Greek Revival style by Thomas Harrison, this Grade II* gem on Mosley Street has been called 'the most refined little building in Manchester.' De Quincey and Gaskell were members. The reading room upstairs still charges an annual subscription. A city library that kept its velvet rope.
_facts: Designed in Greek Revival style by Thomas Harrison of Chester, built 1802-1806
Grade II* listed building, described as 'the most refined little building in Manchester'
Independent subscription library still operating today
Past members included Thomas De Quincey and Elizabeth Gaskell

s: Charles Barry's free gallery: Pre-Raphaelites, Lowry, and civic glory
w: Sir Charles Barry — who rebuilt the Houses of Parliament — designed two of the three buildings here; the earliest is from 1823. The collection runs from Pre-Raphaelite ardour to L.S. Lowry's matchstick crowds. Free to enter, and Manchester's most enduring gift to itself.
_facts: Main gallery premises built for a learned society in 1823
Two of the three connected buildings designed by Sir Charles Barry
Free public art gallery with collections spanning Pre-Raphaelites, L.S. Lowry, and contemporary art
Major renovation and expansion by Hopkins Architects completed in 2002

s: A Manchester Pantheon, opened 1934 and still persuasive
w: E. Vincent Harris designed this rotunda with its columned portico as a loose riff on the Pantheon in Rome, completed in 1934. At its unveiling a critic wrote, 'This is the sort of thing which persuades one to believe in the perennial applicability of the Classical canon.' Manchester civic pride in stone.
_facts: Designed by E. Vincent Harris, constructed 1930-1934
Form derived from the Pantheon, Rome: a columned portico attached to a domed rotunda
Headquarters of Manchester's library and information service
Described at opening as proof of 'the perennial applicability of the Classical canon'

s: Bronze Turing holds an apple in Sackville Gardens — the debt enormous
w: Unveiled in 2001, Glyn Hughes's bronze shows Turing on a bench holding an apple — the fruit tied to both his death and the company that may have borrowed his logo. Turing cracked Enigma and built computing's theoretical foundations at Manchester. The city's formal thanks arrived half a century late.
_facts: Bronze sculpture by Glyn Hughes, unveiled 23 June 2001 in Sackville Gardens
Turing worked at the University of Manchester where the world's first stored-program computer ran in 1948
He was prosecuted for 'gross indecency' in 1952 and received a posthumous royal pardon in 2013
The apple he holds references his death by cyanide poisoning in 1954

s: Five-storey indie bazaar in a former Victorian department store
w: Once Affleck & Brown, one of Manchester's grand Victorian department stores, this Northern Quarter pile reopened in 1982 as a warren of stalls selling vintage, badges, band tees, and tattoos. Every subculture this city produced — punk, rave, goth, indie — passed through its stairwells. Still gloriously chaotic.
_facts: Building was formerly Affleck & Brown, one of Manchester's principal Victorian department stores
Reopened as an indoor market in 1982, now home to dozens of independent stalls and boutiques
Located in the Northern Quarter at the junction of Church Street, Tib Street, and Oldham Street
A bar under the Affleck & Brown name opened on site in 2015

s: The Roman fort that named Manchester, partially rebuilt in the 1980s
w: Founded around AD 79, this Roman auxiliary fort gave Manchester its name. A Scheduled Ancient Monument, its outer walls were partly reconstructed in the 1980s so you can pace a perimeter two millennia old — the Bridgewater Canal below, a Victorian viaduct above. Three layers of transport history stacked at Castlefield.
_facts: Founded c. AD 79 as a Roman castrum in Roman Britain
Garrisoned by a cohort of Roman auxiliaries; civilian settlements developed outside the fort
The fort's name Mamucium or Mancunium is the origin of the city name Manchester
Scheduled Ancient Monument; walls partly reconstructed in the 1980s

s: CAMRA-certified 1888 tile-and-mahogany gem, home of Marble Brewery
w: Designed by Darbyshire and Smith in 1888, this Rochdale Road pub has a mosaic floor, glazed ceramic walls, and a mahogany bar of baffling intricacy — all declared by CAMRA as 'outstanding national historic importance.' Marble Brewery was founded in the back room in 1997 and still brews the house beer.
_facts: Grade II listed pub designed by Darbyshire & Smith, built 1888 on site of McKenna's Brewery show-house
Victorian interior with mosaic floors, glazed ceramic walls, and mahogany fittings
CAMRA recognises the interior as of 'outstanding national historic importance'
Marble Brewery was founded at the rear of the building in 1997

s: Manchester's tallest tower in 1932 — chateau-topped, still the strangest
w: Built in 1932 for solicitor Joseph Sunlight — no relation to the soap — this 14-storey Art Deco tower on Quay Street was Manchester's tallest on completion. The crown is a riot of turrets, dormers, and mansard roofs that look more fairy-tale chateau than office block. Grade II listed and still conspicuous.
_facts: Grade II listed Art Deco building on Quay Street, completed 1932 for Joseph Sunlight
At 14 storeys, it was the tallest building in Manchester when completed
Upper floors feature turrets, dormer windows, and mansard roofs
Joseph Sunlight had no connection to Lever Brothers' Sunlight soap brand

s: Tallest UK office outside London — now entirely wrapped in solar panels
w: Completed in 1962 for the Co-operative Insurance Society, this 118-metre curtain-wall tower was postwar Co-op confidence made concrete. Decades later, maintenance issues prompted a drastic solution: the entire facade was clad in photovoltaic solar panels. Grade II listed; now also a power station.
_facts: Designed by Gordon Tait and G.S. Hay for the Co-operative Insurance Society, completed 1962
Rises 118 metres (387 ft); Grade II listed building
The tallest office building in the UK outside London
Major renovation covered the facade with photovoltaic solar panels

s: A 1421 stone arch swallowed by the city, rediscovered in the 1880s
w: Built in 1421 over Hanging Ditch — a channel linking the Irk and Irwell — this stone arch was slowly engulfed by buildings and forgotten for centuries. Rediscovered in the 1880s, a section is now exposed beside Manchester Cathedral. A Scheduled Ancient Monument, still bearing the weight of six centuries piled on top.
_facts: Medieval arched bridge built in 1421, replacing an earlier bridge at the same location
Spans Hanging Ditch, a watercourse connecting the rivers Irk and Irwell
Completely obscured by buildings over time; rediscovered in the 1880s
Scheduled Ancient Monument; partially exposed section visible near Manchester Cathedral

s: The Victorian terrace where Emmeline Pankhurst founded the WSPU in 1903
w: No. 62 Nelson Street was the Pankhurst family home when Emmeline founded the Women's Social and Political Union on 10 October 1903 — the organisation that would chain itself to railings, smash windows, and force-feed its hunger-strikers into political history. Now a museum and centre for Manchester Women's Aid.
_facts: No. 62 Nelson Street was home to Emmeline Pankhurst and her daughters Sylvia, Christabel, and Adela
The Women's Social and Political Union was founded here on 10 October 1903
Building is now the Pankhurst Museum and headquarters of Manchester Women's Aid
Emmeline Pankhurst received a posthumous statue on St Peter's Square in 2018

s: Peterloo site, Halle home, and where punk germinated in June 1976
w: Built 1853-56 on the Peterloo Massacre site, the Free Trade Hall was home to the Halle Orchestra and hosted, on 4 June 1976, the Sex Pistols gig attended by about 40 people — including future members of Joy Division, The Smiths, and The Fall. Now a Radisson hotel. The ghost of that night is still in the brickwork.
_facts: Constructed 1853-1856 on St Peter's Fields, site of the 1819 Peterloo Massacre
Was the home of the Halle Orchestra, Britain's oldest professional symphony orchestra
Sex Pistols played the Lesser Free Trade Hall on 4 June 1976; audience included future members of Joy Division, The Smiths, and The Fall
Now a Radisson Blu hotel; Grade II* listed building

s: Whisky temple since 1806, 250 malts and a genuine Victorian back snug
w: Established by at least 1806 — appearing in Manchester trade directories for 1821 — this Grade II listed pub on Great Bridgewater Street has barely changed in two centuries. Dark wood, leaded glass, 250 malt whiskies, and a back snug of genuine conspiratorial atmosphere. The name: a Napoleonic-era army recruiting post.
_facts: Grade II listed pub on Great Bridgewater Street, established by at least 1806
Appeared in Pigot and Dean's New Directory of Manchester and Salford for 1821 and 1822
Name refers to its use as a recruiting station for soldiers against Napoleonic invasion
Holds approximately 250 malt whiskies; original Victorian interior fittings survive

s: Abandoned 1893 freight viaduct, now Manchester's High Line
w: Built in 1893 to carry goods to the Great Northern Warehouse, this 330-metre viaduct was abandoned and left to decay. The National Trust acquired it in 2019 and opened it as an elevated public park in 2023. Thirteen arches of Victorian brick now carry wildflower planting and views over Castlefield.
_facts: 330-metre former railway viaduct built in 1893 to serve the Great Northern Warehouse
Stood derelict for decades after the goods line closed
National Trust acquired the viaduct in 2019 and opened it as a public park in 2023
Compared to New York's High Line; views over Castlefield canal basin

s: Owen Williams's 1939 glass-and-vitrolite streamliner, still arrestingly modern
w: Engineer Sir Owen Williams designed three identical glass-and-black-vitrolite buildings for the Daily Express in 1939 — London, Glasgow, and this one on Great Ancoats Street. Horizontal glazing bands and curved corners make it among the finest pre-war streamline Modernist structures in England. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Grade II* listed building designed by engineer Sir Owen Williams, built 1939
One of three identical Daily Express regional press buildings (London, Glasgow, Manchester)
Notable for its glass curtain wall and black vitrolite cladding on Great Ancoats Street
Williams also engineered Wembley Stadium and the Boots factory in Nottingham

s: Free hospital for the poor since 1828, in the world's first industrial suburb
w: The Ardwick and Ancoats Dispensary opened in 1828 to serve workers in what was then the world's most densely packed industrial suburb — cotton mills and foundries around the Rochdale Canal. The current Grade II listed building replaced it in 1875. It survived decades of dereliction; Ancoats is now a conservation area.
_facts: The Ardwick and Ancoats Dispensary first opened in 1828 as free medical care for local workers
Current Grade II listed building built in 1875 to replace the original
Ancoats is considered the world's first industrial suburb
The building stood derelict for many years before restoration began

s: The UK's national museum of labour history, in a hydraulic pumping station
w: The nation's only museum of working-class history occupies a former hydraulic pumping station designed by city architect Henry Price. The Grade II listed building once pushed pressurised water through pipes to power cranes and lifts across Manchester. Now: Britain's oldest surviving trade union banner, suffragette material, and the Chartists.
_facts: UK's national centre for the history of working people in Britain
Located in a Grade II listed former hydraulic pumping station designed by Henry Price
The station supplied pressurised water to power cranes and lifts across the city
Collections include Britain's oldest surviving trade union banner (1821) and Chartist and suffragette material

s: 1874 Sephardic synagogue on Cheetham Hill, now a museum of Jewish Manchester
w: Built in 1874 for the Congregation of Spanish and Portuguese Jews — Sephardic exiles whose community in England dated back to the 17th century — this ornate synagogue served the families who built much of Manchester's textile trade. Restored and reopened in 2021 with a gallery tracing 350 years of Jewish life in the city.
_facts: Former Spanish and Portuguese Synagogue (Sha'are Tephillah), built 1874 for a Sephardic congregation
Cheetham Hill was the heart of Manchester's Jewish community in the Victorian era
Now the Manchester Jewish Museum, tracing 350 years of Jewish life in the city
Underwent major restoration, reopening in 2021 with expanded galleries

s: World's oldest surviving terminal station, opened September 1830
w: On 15 September 1830 the first passengers arrived here from Liverpool on the world's first inter-city railway with timetabled steam haulage. The building is the oldest surviving terminal station on earth. Tracks still run at first-floor level behind it — possibly the world's first elevated railway too. Now the Science and Industry Museum's centrepiece.
_facts: Opened 15 September 1830 as the Manchester terminus of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway
World's first inter-city passenger railway with timetabled steam haulage
The world's oldest surviving terminal railway station
Tracks at first-floor level; now part of the Science and Industry Museum
